name: ipython-genutils
documentation: Pretend this doesn't exist. Nobody should use it.

modules.name: ipython_genutils.text
documentation:
Utilities for working with strings and text.

Inheritance diagram:

```
.. inheritance-diagram:: IPython.utils.text
   :parts: 3
```
encoding: utf-8

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text.indent
documentation:
Indent a string a given number of spaces or tabstops.

indent(str,nspaces=4,ntabs=0) -> indent str by ntabs+nspaces.

Parameters
----------

instr : basestring
    The string to be indented.
nspaces : int (default: 4)
    The number of spaces to be indented.
ntabs : int (default: 0)
    The number of tabs to be indented.
flatten : bool (default: False)
    Whether to scrub existing indentation.  If True, all lines will be
    aligned to the same indentation.  If False, existing indentation will
    be strictly increased.

Returns
-------

str|unicode : string indented by ntabs and nspaces.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text.dedent
documentation:
Equivalent of textwrap.dedent that ignores unindented first line.

This means it will still dedent strings like:
'''foo
is a bar
'''

For use in wrap_paragraphs.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text.wrap_paragraphs
documentation:
Wrap multiple paragraphs to fit a specified width.

This is equivalent to textwrap.wrap, but with support for multiple
paragraphs, as separated by empty lines.

Returns
-------

list of complete paragraphs, wrapped to fill `ncols` columns.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text.strip_ansi
documentation:
Remove ansi escape codes from text.

Parameters
----------
source : str
    Source to remove the ansi from

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text._chunks
documentation: Yield successive n-sized chunks from l.
Utils to columnize a list of string

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text._find_optimal
documentation: Calculate optimal info to columnize a list of string

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text._get_or_default
documentation: return list item number, or default if don't exist

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text.compute_item_matrix
documentation:
Returns a nested list, and info to columnize items

# Parameters

items

: list of strings to columize

empty

: default value to fill list if needed

separator_size

: How much caracters will be used as a separation between each columns.

displaywidth

: The width of the area onto wich the columns should enter

# Returns

strings_matrix

> nested list of string, the outer most list contains as many list as
> rows, the innermost lists have each as many element as colums. If the
> total number of elements in `items` does not equal the product of
> rows\*columns, the last element of some lists are filled with `None`.

dict_info

: some info to make columnize easier:

  columns_numbers

  : number of columns

  rows_numbers

  : number of rows

  columns_width

  : list of with of each columns

  optimal_separator_width

  : best separator width between columns

# Examples

```
In [1]: l = ['aaa','b','cc','d','eeeee','f','g','h','i','j','k','l']
   ...: compute_item_matrix(l,displaywidth=12)
Out[1]:
    ([['aaa', 'f', 'k'],
    ['b', 'g', 'l'],
    ['cc', 'h', None],
    ['d', 'i', None],
    ['eeeee', 'j', None]],
    {'columns_numbers': 3,
    'columns_width': [5, 1, 1],
    'optimal_separator_width': 2,
    'rows_numbers': 5})
```

functions.name: ipython_genutils.text.columnize
documentation:
Transform a list of strings into a single string with columns.

Parameters
----------
items : sequence of strings
    The strings to process.

separator : str, optional [default is two spaces]
    The string that separates columns.

displaywidth : int, optional [default is 80]
    Width of the display in number of characters.

Returns
-------
The formatted string.

modules.name: ipython_genutils.tempdir
documentation:
TemporaryDirectory class, copied from Python 3

This is copied from the stdlib and will be standard in Python 3.2 and onwards.

classes.name: ipython_genutils.tempdir.TemporaryDirectory
documentation:
Create and return a temporary directory.  This has the same
behavior as mkdtemp but can be used as a context manager.  For
example:

    with TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
        ...

Upon exiting the context, the directory and everthing contained
in it are removed.

classes.name: ipython_genutils.tempdir.NamedFileInTemporaryDirectory

methods.name: ipython_genutils.tempdir.NamedFileInTemporaryDirectory.__init__
documentation:
Open a file named `filename` in a temporary directory.

This context manager is preferred over `NamedTemporaryFile` in
stdlib `tempfile` when one needs to reopen the file.

Arguments `mode` and `bufsize` are passed to `open`.
Rest of the arguments are passed to `TemporaryDirectory`.
extra temp-dir-related context managers

classes.name: ipython_genutils.tempdir.TemporaryWorkingDirectory
documentation:
Creates a temporary directory and sets the cwd to that directory.
Automatically reverts to previous cwd upon cleanup.
Usage example:

    with TemporaryWorkingDirectory() as tmpdir:
        ...

modules.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct
documentation:
A dict subclass that supports attribute style access.

Can probably be replaced by types.SimpleNamespace from Python 3.3
encoding: utf-8

classes.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__init__
documentation:
Initialize with a dictionary, another Struct, or data.

# Parameters

args

: Initialize with one dict or Struct

kw

: Initialize with key, value pairs.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s.a
10
>>> s.b
30
>>> s2 = Struct(s,c=30)
>>> sorted(s2.keys())
['a', 'b', 'c']
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__setitem__
documentation:
Set an item with check for allownew.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Struct()
>>> s['a'] = 10
>>> s.allow_new_attr(False)
>>> s['a'] = 10
>>> s['a']
10
>>> try:
...     s['b'] = 20
... except KeyError:
...     print('this is not allowed')
...
this is not allowed
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__setattr__
documentation:
Set an attr with protection of class members.

This calls {meth}`self.__setitem__` but convert {exc}`KeyError` to
{exc}`AttributeError`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Struct()
>>> s.a = 10
>>> s.a
10
>>> try:
...     s.get = 10
... except AttributeError:
...     print("you can't set a class member")
...
you can't set a class member
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__getattr__
documentation:
Get an attr by calling {meth}`dict.__getitem__`.

Like {meth}`__setattr__`, this method converts {exc}`KeyError` to
{exc}`AttributeError`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Struct(a=10)
>>> s.a
10
>>> type(s.get)
<... 'builtin_function_or_method'>
>>> try:
...     s.b
... except AttributeError:
...     print("I don't have that key")
...
I don't have that key
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__iadd__
documentation:
s += s2 is a shorthand for s.merge(s2).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s2 = Struct(a=20,c=40)
>>> s += s2
>>> sorted(s.keys())
['a', 'b', 'c']
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__add__
documentation:
s + s2 -> New Struct made from s.merge(s2).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s1 = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s2 = Struct(a=20,c=40)
>>> s = s1 + s2
>>> sorted(s.keys())
['a', 'b', 'c']
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__sub__
documentation:
s1 - s2 -> remove keys in s2 from s1.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s1 = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s2 = Struct(a=40)
>>> s = s1 - s2
>>> s
{'b': 30}
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__isub__
documentation:
Inplace remove keys from self that are in other.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s1 = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s2 = Struct(a=40)
>>> s1 -= s2
>>> s1
{'b': 30}
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.__dict_invert
documentation:
Helper function for merge.

Takes a dictionary whose values are lists and returns a dict with
the elements of each list as keys and the original keys as values.

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.copy
documentation:
Return a copy as a Struct.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s2 = s.copy()
>>> type(s2) is Struct
True
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.hasattr
documentation:
hasattr function available as a method.

Implemented like has_key.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> s = Struct(a=10)
>>> s.hasattr('a')
True
>>> s.hasattr('b')
False
>>> s.hasattr('get')
False
```

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.allow_new_attr
documentation:
Set whether new attributes can be created in this Struct.

This can be used to catch typos by verifying that the attribute user
tries to change already exists in this Struct.

methods.name: ipython_genutils.ipstruct.Struct.merge
documentation:
Merge two Structs with customizable conflict resolution.

This is similar to {meth}`update`, but much more flexible. First, a
dict is made from data+key=value pairs. When merging this dict with
the Struct S, the optional dictionary 'conflict' is used to decide
what to do.

If conflict is not given, the default behavior is to preserve any keys
with their current value (the opposite of the {meth}`update` method's
behavior).

# Parameters

\_\_loc_data

: The data to merge into self

\_\_conflict_solve

: The conflict policy dict. The keys are binary functions used to
  resolve the conflict and the values are lists of strings naming
  the keys the conflict resolution function applies to. Instead of
  a list of strings a space separated string can be used, like
  'a b c'.

kw

: Additional key, value pairs to merge in

# Notes

The `__conflict_solve` dict is a dictionary of binary functions which will be used to
solve key conflicts. Here is an example:

```
__conflict_solve = dict(
    func1=['a','b','c'],
    func2=['d','e']
)
```

In this case, the function {func}`func1` will be used to resolve
keys 'a', 'b' and 'c' and the function {func}`func2` will be used for
keys 'd' and 'e'. This could also be written as:

```
__conflict_solve = dict(func1='a b c',func2='d e')
```

These functions will be called for each key they apply to with the
form:

```
func1(self['a'], other['a'])
```

The return value is used as the final merged value.

As a convenience, merge() provides five (the most commonly needed)
pre-defined policies: preserve, update, add, add_flip and add_s. The
easiest explanation is their implementation:

```
preserve = lambda old,new: old
update   = lambda old,new: new
add      = lambda old,new: old + new
add_flip = lambda old,new: new + old  # note change of order!
add_s    = lambda old,new: old + ' ' + new  # only for str!
```

You can use those four words (as strings) as keys instead
of defining them as functions, and the merge method will substitute
the appropriate functions for you.

For more complicated conflict resolution policies, you still need to
construct your own functions.

# Examples

This show the default policy:

```pycon
>>> s = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s2 = Struct(a=20,c=40)
>>> s.merge(s2)
>>> sorted(s.items())
[('a', 10), ('b', 30), ('c', 40)]
```

Now, show how to specify a conflict dict:

```pycon
>>> s = Struct(a=10,b=30)
>>> s2 = Struct(a=20,b=40)
>>> conflict = {'update':'a','add':'b'}
>>> s.merge(s2,conflict)
>>> sorted(s.items())
[('a', 20), ('b', 70)]
```

A dict subclass with attribute style access.

This dict subclass has a a few extra features:

* Attribute style access.
* Protection of class members (like keys, items) when using attribute
  style access.
* The ability to restrict assignment to only existing keys.
* Intelligent merging.
* Overloaded operators.

modules.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators
documentation:
Decorators for labeling test objects.

Decorators that merely return a modified version of the original function
object are straightforward.  Decorators that return a new function object need
to use nose.tools.make_decorator(original_function)(decorator) in returning the
decorator, in order to preserve metadata such as function name, setup and
teardown functions and so on - see nose.tools for more information.

This module provides a set of useful decorators meant to be ready to use in
your own tests.  See the bottom of the file for the ready-made ones, and if you
find yourself writing a new one that may be of generic use, add it here.

Included decorators:


Lightweight testing that remains unittest-compatible.

- An @as_unittest decorator can be used to tag any normal parameter-less
  function as a unittest TestCase.  Then, both nose and normal unittest will
  recognize it as such.  This will make it easier to migrate away from Nose if
  we ever need/want to while maintaining very lightweight tests.

NOTE: This file contains IPython-specific decorators. Using the machinery in
IPython.external.decorators, we import either numpy.testing.decorators if numpy is
available, OR use equivalent code in IPython.external._decorators, which
we've copied verbatim from numpy.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.as_unittest
documentation: Decorator to make a simple function into a normal test via unittest.
Classes and functions
Simple example of the basic idea

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.make_label_dec
documentation:
Factory function to create a decorator that applies one or more labels.

# Parameters

> label : string or sequence
> One or more labels that will be applied by the decorator to the functions

it decorates. Labels are attributes of the decorated function with their
value set to True.

> ds : string
> An optional docstring for the resulting decorator. If not given, a
> default docstring is auto-generated.

# Returns

> A decorator.

# Examples

A simple labeling decorator:

```pycon
>>> slow = make_label_dec('slow')
>>> slow.__doc__
"Labels a test as 'slow'."
```

And one that uses multiple labels and a custom docstring:

```pycon
>>> rare = make_label_dec(['slow','hard'],
... "Mix labels 'slow' and 'hard' for rare tests.")
>>> rare.__doc__
"Mix labels 'slow' and 'hard' for rare tests."
```

Now, let's test using this one:
\>>> @rare
... def f(): pass
...
\>>>
\>>> f.slow
True
\>>> f.hard
True
Utility functions

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.skipif
documentation:
Make function raise SkipTest exception if skip_condition is true

Parameters
----------

skip_condition : bool or callable
  Flag to determine whether to skip test. If the condition is a
  callable, it is used at runtime to dynamically make the decision. This
  is useful for tests that may require costly imports, to delay the cost
  until the test suite is actually executed.
msg : string
  Message to give on raising a SkipTest exception.

Returns
-------
decorator : function
  Decorator, which, when applied to a function, causes SkipTest
  to be raised when the skip_condition was True, and the function
  to be called normally otherwise.

Notes
-----
You will see from the code that we had to further decorate the
decorator with the nose.tools.make_decorator function in order to
transmit function name, and various other metadata.
Inspired by numpy's skipif, but uses the full apply_wrapper utility to
preserve function metadata better and allows the skip condition to be a
callable.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.skip
documentation:
Decorator factory - mark a test function for skipping from test suite.

Parameters
----------
  msg : string
    Optional message to be added.

Returns
-------
   decorator : function
     Decorator, which, when applied to a function, causes SkipTest
     to be raised, with the optional message added.
A version with the condition set to true, common case just to attach a message
to a skip decorator

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.onlyif
documentation: The reverse from skipif, see skipif for details.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.module_not_available
documentation:
Can module be imported?  Returns true if module does NOT import.

This is used to make a decorator to skip tests that require module to be
available, but delay the 'import numpy' to test execution time.
Utility functions for decorators

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.decorated_dummy
documentation:
Return a dummy function decorated with dec, with the given name.

Examples
--------
import IPython.testing.decorators as dec
setup = dec.decorated_dummy(dec.skip_if_no_x11, __name__)

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.skip_file_no_x11
documentation: not a decorator itself, returns a dummy function to be used as setup

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.onlyif_cmds_exist
documentation: Decorator to skip test when at least one of `commands` is not found.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.testing.decorators.onlyif_any_cmd_exists
documentation: Decorator to skip test unless at least one of `commands` is found.

modules.name: ipython_genutils.encoding
documentation: Utilities for dealing with text encodings
coding: utf-8

functions.name: ipython_genutils.encoding.get_stream_enc
documentation:
Return the given stream's encoding or a default.

There are cases where `sys.std*` might not actually be a stream, so
check for the encoding attribute prior to returning it, and return
a default if it doesn't exist or evaluates as False. `default`
is None if not provided.
to deal with the possibility of sys.std* not being a stream at all

functions.name: ipython_genutils.encoding.getdefaultencoding
documentation:
Return IPython's guess for the default encoding for bytes as text.

If prefer_stream is True (default), asks for stdin.encoding first,
to match the calling Terminal, but that is often None for subprocesses.

Then fall back on locale.getpreferredencoding(),
which should be a sensible platform default (that respects LANG environment),
and finally to sys.getdefaultencoding() which is the most conservative option,
and usually ASCII on Python 2 or UTF8 on Python 3.
Less conservative replacement for sys.getdefaultencoding, that will try
to match the environment.
Defined here as central function, so if we find better choices, we
won't need to make changes all over IPython.

modules.name: ipython_genutils.importstring
documentation: A simple utility to import something by its string name.
encoding: utf-8

functions.name: ipython_genutils.importstring.import_item
documentation:
Import and return `bar` given the string `foo.bar`.

Calling `bar = import_item("foo.bar")` is the functional equivalent of
executing the code `from foo import bar`.

# Parameters

name

: The fully qualified name of the module/package being imported.

# Returns

mod

: The module that was imported.
Copyright (c) IPython Development Team.
Distributed under the terms of the Modified BSD License.

modules.name: ipython_genutils.path
documentation: Utilities for path handling.
encoding: utf-8

functions.name: ipython_genutils.path.filefind
documentation:
Find a file by looking through a sequence of paths.

This iterates through a sequence of paths looking for a file and returns
the full, absolute path of the first occurence of the file. If no set of
path dirs is given, the filename is tested as is, after running through
{func}`expandvars` and {func}`expanduser`. Thus a simple call:

```
filefind('myfile.txt')
```

will find the file in the current working dir, but:

```
filefind('~/myfile.txt')
```

Will find the file in the users home directory. This function does not
automatically try any paths, such as the cwd or the user's home directory.

# Parameters

filename

: The filename to look for.

path_dirs

: The sequence of paths to look for the file in. If None, the filename
  need to be absolute or be in the cwd. If a string, the string is
  put into a sequence and the searched. If a sequence, walk through
  each element and join with `filename`, calling {func}`expandvars`
  and {func}`expanduser` before testing for existence.

# Returns

Raises {exc}`IOError` or returns absolute path to file.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.path.expand_path
documentation:
Expand $VARS and ~names in a string, like a shell

:Examples:

   In [2]: os.environ['FOO']='test'

   In [3]: expand_path('variable FOO is $FOO')
   Out[3]: 'variable FOO is test'

functions.name: ipython_genutils.path.link
documentation:
Hard links `src` to `dst`, returning 0 or errno.

Note that the special errno `ENOLINK` will be returned if `os.link` isn't
supported by the operating system.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.path.link_or_copy
documentation:
Attempts to hardlink `src` to `dst`, copying if the link fails.

Attempts to maintain the semantics of `shutil.copy`.

Because `os.link` does not overwrite files, a unique temporary file
will be used if the target already exists, then that file will be moved
into place.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.path.ensure_dir_exists
documentation:
ensure that a directory exists

If it doesn't exist, try to create it and protect against a race condition
if another process is doing the same.

The default permissions are 755, which differ from os.makedirs default of 777.

modules.name: ipython_genutils.py3compat
documentation: Compatibility tricks for Python 3. Mainly to do with unicode.
coding: utf-8

functions.name: ipython_genutils.py3compat.buffer_to_bytes
documentation: Cast a buffer or memoryview object to bytes

functions.name: ipython_genutils.py3compat.safe_unicode
documentation:
unicode(e) with various fallbacks. Used for exceptions, which may not be
safe to call unicode() on.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.py3compat._shutil_which
documentation:
Given a command, mode, and a PATH string, return the path which
conforms to the given mode on the PATH, or None if there is no such
file.

`mode` defaults to os.F_OK | os.X_OK. `path` defaults to the result
of os.environ.get("PATH"), or can be overridden with a custom search
path.

This is a backport of shutil.which from Python 3.4
shutil.which from Python 3.4

functions.name: ipython_genutils.py3compat.input
documentation: keep reference to builtin_mod because the kernel overrides that value
to forward requests to a frontend.

functions.name: ipython_genutils.py3compat.doctest_refactor_print
documentation:
Refactor 'print x' statements in a doctest to print(x) style. 2to3
unfortunately doesn't pick up on our doctests.

Can accept a string or a function, so it can be used as a decorator.
